quick_index.title: Quick Index
quick_index.content:
- Purpose
- Target Profile
- Default Start
- Task Lifecycle
- Phase 1 — Read
- Phase 2 — Decide
- Phase 3 — Plan
- Phase 4 — Implement
- Phase 5 — Report
- Phase 6 — Test
- Phase 7 — Joint Verification
- Phase 8 — Handoff
- Communication Profile

purpose.title: Purpose
purpose.content:
🧭 Work model for foreign or legacy business codebases.

Applies when the agent does not fully know the domain, the codebase is messy, framework conventions are inconsistent or missing, test coverage is partial or absent, and the user is the domain reviewer plus tester.

Language-agnostic and company-agnostic. Use for any work where domain understanding lags codebase touch: inherited monoliths, customizations of CRM/ERP/billing/business platforms, integration glue, business-rules engines, legacy framework projects, and similar.

This profile sets a fixed 8-phase task lifecycle, a business-storytelling end-of-task report, and explicit unknown-unknown surfacing rules. It is neither the default code-pack workflow nor the gamedev workflow — it is a third, narrower work model for a specific situation.

target_profile.title: Target Profile
target_profile.content:
🎯 Activate this work model when ANY of the following hold:

- Domain knowledge belongs primarily to the user, not the agent
- Codebase has limited or no automated tests
- Framework conventions vary across the project
- New code mixes with legacy patterns in the same files
- User is primary reviewer and primary tester
- Agent must propose manual test scenarios for UI clicks, OAuth, prod-only paths, third-party integrations
- Tasks are tracked in an external system (Jira, Linear, Asana, ServiceNow, etc.)
- Pull requests, deployment plans, and ticket lifecycle are user-owned

Do NOT activate when:

- Greenfield project where agent owns architecture and conventions
- Well-tested codebase with stable, enforced conventions
- Game engine or simulation work — use SocratexGamedev profile instead
- Default clean code-pack workflow is sufficient

default_start.title: Default Start
default_start.content:
📌 For any task in this profile, read project STATE first, then route by trigger.

When the user provides an external task URL or names a ticket: trigger the 8-phase task lifecycle below.

When the user asks a direct question or names a small fix without ticket context: skip to Phase 4 (Implement) but keep Phase 5 (Report) and Phase 6 (Test) mandatory.

Keep `STATE.json` and `<branch>-STATE.json` updated as work progresses, not at end.

task_lifecycle.title: Task Lifecycle Overview
task_lifecycle.content:
🔁 Eight phases. Linear with one branch: easy single-file fix skips phase 3.

1. Read — fetch entire task and metadata, including linked tickets and referenced documents
2. Decide — classify complexity
3. Plan — only if multi-step or architectural; research-first before custom design
4. Implement — execute per plan; user reviews
5. Report — business storytelling, no jargon
6. Test — auto where safe, manual scenarios where not
7. Joint verification — user runs manual, agent runs automated
8. Handoff — user does commit/push/PR/deployment-plan/ticket-close; agent prepares paste-ready draft text only

phase_1_read.title: Phase 1 — Read
phase_1_read.content:
📚 Read entire task and all reachable context before deciding anything.

- Full task description, acceptance criteria, comments, attachments, metadata
- All linked tickets — blocks, blocked-by, relates-to, parent/child
- All referenced internal documents — design docs, audit notes, runbooks, ADRs
- If task references inaccessible external docs (intranet wiki, shared drive, private notes) — flag the gap per no-fabrication rule, ask user to share before proceeding

Do not start designing or coding while context is incomplete.

phase_2_decide.title: Phase 2 — Decide complexity
phase_2_decide.content:
⚖️ Two outcomes only.

- Easy single-file fix with clear ownership → skip to Phase 4
- Multi-step, architectural, multi-file, or unclear scope → Phase 3 first

When in doubt, prefer Phase 3 — plan cost is low, missed-plan cost is high.

phase_3_plan.title: Phase 3 — Plan (when needed)
phase_3_plan.content:
📐 Research-first, no custom design before known approaches are checked.

See `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` → Primary Known-Solutions Directive for general philosophy.

Decide implementation shape from these archetypes:

- `surgical change` — touch minimal code in current file, match surrounding style
- `extract to helper/class` — new code does not belong to current file's business domain
- `clean domain module (e.g. DDD+ADIV)` — when architecture supports domain modeling and change is large enough
- `full rewrite of touched module` — only when surgical/extract would create more debt than rewrite

Decision drivers:

- Change size — small → surgical; large → extract or rewrite
- Business context fit — does new code still belong in current file's business domain? yes → in-place; no → extract
- Predictable load — will this scale (count, frequency, I/O, storage)? yes → light helper now, not retrofit later

Ask the user about lightweight-futureproof when scope or future load is unclear.

Save PLAN to `<branch>-PLAN.json` and update STATE before implementation begins.

phase_4_implement.title: Phase 4 — Implement
phase_4_implement.content:
✏️ Execute per PLAN.

- Match surrounding code style in legacy areas; avoid paradigm migration during narrow fixes
- Minimize blast radius unless plan explicitly says broader refactor is in scope
- Update STATE with implementation progress

User reviews code — read, review, fix, standardize.
Agent does not commit; agent does not push.

phase_5_report.title: Phase 5 — Report (business storytelling)
phase_5_report.content:
🗣️ Two compact sections, business language, no technical jargon.

- **What was needed / what was the problem** — simplified, domain-level
- **What I did** — simplified, domain-level

The storytelling layer goes into chat reply AND `<branch>-STATE.json` `session_handoff_*` so the user can paste it into PR description, ticket comment, deployment plan, or release notes.

Technical detail stays in code diff and STATE technical sections — not in the storytelling.

phase_6_test.title: Phase 6 — Test
phase_6_test.content:
🧪 Devise test plan first, then execute what is safe.

Safe to test alone — execute now and report:

- API call to local or staging endpoint
- Unit test suite, integration test suite
- Linter, type-checker, static analyzer
- Isolated smoke test that does not mutate shared state

Requires user interaction — write manual scenario into PLAN:

- Where to click or what to call
- What input to use
- What signal indicates success
- What signal indicates failure
- Expected before/after state

Update STATE with results or test scenario.

phase_7_joint_verification.title: Phase 7 — Joint verification
phase_7_joint_verification.content:
🤝 User runs manual scenarios, agent runs automated, both report.

- Iterate fixes if signals fail
- Re-test after each fix
- STATE reflects current verified status

Do not close the task while any signal is failing or unverified.

phase_8_handoff.title: Phase 8 — Handoff
phase_8_handoff.content:
📤 User does all write actions on shared systems.

User does:

- Commit, push, create PR
- Deployment plan in Confluence or equivalent shared doc
- Ticket comment, transition, close

Agent prepares all paste-ready draft text in `<branch>-STATE.json` `session_handoff_*`:

- PR description body
- Ticket comment body
- Deployment plan draft
- Release notes if applicable

Agent never initiates PR, ticket, or deployment lifecycle. Agent never runs PR-create CLI calls, ticket write MCP tools, or write API calls to shared systems.

communication_profile.title: Communication Profile
communication_profile.content:
💬 Default communication profile for this work model: `foreign_legacy_code`.

Set `communication.profile` to `foreign_legacy_code` in `PIPELINE-CONFIG.json` to activate.

See `core/communication-profiles/foreign_legacy_code.txt` for full style rules. Key additions over `epistemic`:

- Business storytelling layer at end-of-task (Phase 5 report)
- Explicit unknown-unknown surfacing — do not extrapolate from name conventions, imports, or surrounding comments as if they were documentation
- Test scenarios for what cannot be safely auto-tested

behavioral_rules.title: Behavioral Rules
behavioral_rules.content:
🛡️ Three hard rules specific to foreign/legacy work.

**Rule 1 — Domain unknowns**
Agent does not know the business domain by default. When asked about "what should this do", "why does X exist", "what is the rule for Y": flag domain unknown, ask the user, do not extrapolate from code names or comments unless they are explicit specifications.

**Rule 2 — Test ownership**
Agent tests what is safely auto-testable. Agent writes manual test scenarios for UI clicks, OAuth flows, prod-only paths, third-party integrations, side-effecting writes. Agent does not skip Phase 6 just because no automated tests exist.

**Rule 3 — Lifecycle ownership**
User owns commit, push, PR creation, ticket lifecycle, and deployment plans. Agent prepares draft text for paste. Agent never initiates these actions, even when permissions would technically allow it.

routing.purpose: Work model for legacy or unfamiliar business codebases — 8-phase task lifecycle with business storytelling, explicit unknown-unknown surfacing, user-owned lifecycle handoff.
read_when: When PIPELINE-CONFIG.json sets the active project profile reference to SocratexForeignLegacy.
When the task is foreign/legacy business code and the user expects business storytelling plus user-owned PR/ticket lifecycle.
do_not_read_when: When the project is greenfield with agent-owned architecture.
When the project is game engine or simulation work — use SocratexGamedev profile instead.
When the default clean code-pack workflow is sufficient.
